sections.subtitle: Current Form
sections.text: Brighton & Hove Albion have shown decent form in their last five matches, securing 3 wins, 1 draw, and 1 loss, with a strong attacking output of 10 goals. West Ham United, on the other hand, have struggled, managing only 2 wins, 2 draws, and 1 loss, while conceding in every game, highlighting defensive vulnerabilities.

sections.subtitle: Head-to-Head Record
sections.text: In their last two encounters, Brighton have the edge with 1 win and 1 draw against West Ham, scoring 4 goals to West Ham's 3. Their most recent clash at Brighton’s home ended in a thrilling 3-2 victory for the Seagulls, showcasing their ability to capitalize on chances late in the game.

sections.subtitle: Season Statistics
sections.text: Brighton rank 5th in the league for goals scored (24), with a high conversion rate of big chances (54.8%), and excel in pressing (1st in possessions won in the final third). West Ham, languishing at 18th with only 16 goals and 28 conceded, struggle defensively, as evidenced by no clean sheets in their last 11 matches.

sections.subtitle: Key Players
sections.text: Brighton’s Danny Welbeck (7 goals, 3rd in league) and Jan Paul van Hecke (3 goals, recent form) are in fine shape to exploit West Ham’s leaky defense. For West Ham, Jarrod Bowen (3 goals) and Lucas Paquetá (3 goals) offer attacking threat, but their impact may be limited by Brighton’s robust tackling (1st in league).

sections.subtitle: Tactical Insights
sections.text: Brighton’s ability to win possession in the final third and create big chances could dominate West Ham’s shaky backline. West Ham’s reliance on late goals (high percentage in 76-90 minutes) might not suffice against Brighton’s structured defense and home advantage.

sections.subtitle: Match Outlook
sections.text: Given Sofascore’s prediction (55% home win, 21% draw, 24% away win) and Brighton’s superior form, home record, and statistical edge, they are favorites to win. West Ham’s ongoing defensive issues and lack of clean sheets further tilt the balance in Brighton’s favor.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Overall first half summary
halftimeSections.text: The first half at the American Express Stadium between Brighton & Hove Albion and West Ham United ended in a goalless draw, with both teams showing moments of promise but failing to capitalize on their opportunities. Brighton dominated possession and created more entries into the final third and penalty area, indicating a more assertive approach in attack. However, their inability to convert a big chance missed reflects a lack of clinical finishing. West Ham, while less dominant in possession, managed to create a significant opportunity of their own but similarly failed to find the net. The match remains finely balanced as both sides head into the break.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Brighton's first half performance
halftimeSections.text: Brighton & Hove Albion controlled much of the game's tempo, showcasing their intent through frequent forays into the opposition's final third and penalty area. Their aerial dominance and numerous corner kicks suggest a strong physical presence, though they struggled to turn this pressure into shots on target. A missed big chance highlights a need for sharper finishing, with players like Mats Wieffer showing potential threat but unable to convert. Defensively, Brighton held firm, with key contributions in clearances and interceptions, but they will need to be more decisive in front of goal to break the deadlock.

halftimeSections.subtitle: West Ham's first half performance
halftimeSections.text: West Ham United adopted a more measured approach in the first half, conceding possession to Brighton but staying competitive in duels, particularly on the ground. Their defensive structure was solid, with multiple clearances helping to neutralize Brighton's attacking forays. Offensively, they created a big chance but failed to capitalize, mirroring their opponents' struggles in the final third. Players like Lucas Paquetá showed creativity with key passes, suggesting West Ham have the tools to unlock Brighton if they can sustain pressure in the second half.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Attack momentum analysis
halftimeSections.text: The first half saw Brighton starting with strong attacking intent, pushing forward with consistent spells of pressure in the early stages. Their momentum was particularly notable in the middle and latter parts of the half, with prolonged periods of dominance in the opposition half. West Ham, on the other hand, had their moments, with noticeable shifts in momentum during specific intervals where they mounted sustained attacks. While Brighton's attacking waves were more frequent, West Ham's bursts showed they can be dangerous on the counter or during set-piece scenarios.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Key players to watch
halftimeSections.text: For Brighton, Mats Wieffer stands out as a potential game-changer in the second half due to his involvement in a big chance missed and his ability to get into threatening positions. Maxim De Cuyper also impressed with creative contributions through key passes and chance creation. On the West Ham side, Lucas Paquetá's vision and ability to create opportunities could be pivotal if his teammates can finish the chances he sets up. Crysencio Summerville, despite missing a big chance, remains a threat with his willingness to take on defenders and shoot.
